title: Greedy Algorithms
description: Making the locally best choice at every step and hoping it adds up to the best overall answer — plus the important caveat of when that hope fails.
objectives: Understand what a greedy algorithm is
Recognize when a greedy approach gives the correct answer
Understand a concrete case where greedy fails

sections.text: A greedy algorithm builds a solution by always making the choice that looks best at the current step, without reconsidering that choice later, hoping the sequence of locally best choices adds up to a globally best result.
2. Classic Example: Minimum Coins for Change

sections.title: Tracing it
points: amount=41: take 25 (largest that fits) → amount=16, count=1
amount=16: 25 too big; take 10 → amount=6, count=2
Result: 4 coins — and this genuinely IS the minimum possible for this coin system

sections.text: This is the critical caveat: greedy's "take the biggest coin now" reasoning gave a WRONG (non-optimal) answer here, because taking 4 first left a remainder (2) that couldn't be efficiently completed. Greedy happens to work for common real-world coin denominations like {25, 10, 5, 1}, but it is not guaranteed to work for every possible coin system — and it doesn't. Recognizing which problems greedy actually solves correctly is the hard, important part.

sections.title: Packing a suitcase by always grabbing the most valuable item first
sections.text: Packing by always grabbing the single most valuable remaining item that still fits sounds reasonable, but it can leave awkward, wasted space at the end — a smarter combination of slightly less "individually valuable" items might have used the space better overall. That's exactly the risk of greedy reasoning: locally reasonable choices don't always add up to the best possible outcome.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming greedy always gives the correct answer just because it's simple and intuitive — it must be proven correct for the specific problem, as the coin example shows.
Applying the coin-change greedy solution to an arbitrary set of denominations — it only works correctly for certain ('canonical') coin systems, not all of them.
Confusing 'a reasonable-sounding strategy' with 'a proven greedy algorithm' — real greedy algorithms come with a justification for why the locally best choice never hurts the final answer.
Forgetting greedy makes each choice permanently — unlike Backtracking, there's no reconsidering an earlier decision once it's made.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a greedy algorithm?
questions.answer: An algorithm that always makes the choice that looks best at the current step, without reconsidering it later, hoping the sequence of locally best choices produces the globally best result.

questions.question: Does the greedy 'always take the largest coin' strategy work for every possible set of coin denominations?
questions.answer: No — it works for common denominations like {25, 10, 5, 1}, but can give a non-optimal answer for other coin systems, such as {4, 3, 1} when making 6 cents.

questions.question: What is the key risk of using a greedy approach?
questions.answer: A sequence of locally optimal choices isn't guaranteed to add up to the globally optimal solution — greedy must be proven correct for the specific problem, not just assumed to work.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A greedy algorithm builds a solution by always making the locally best-looking choice at each step, without revisiting that decision later. For making change with common denominations like {25, 10, 5, 1}, always taking the largest coin that fits genuinely produces the minimum number of coins. But greedy isn't universally correct — with a different coin system like {4, 3, 1}, greedily taking a 4 first when making 6 cents gives 3 coins (4+1+1), while the true optimal answer is 2 coins (3+3). This is the critical caveat: a greedy strategy must be proven correct for the specific problem, since locally reasonable choices don't always add up to the globally best outcome.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Greedy Algorithms
sections.text: Greedy = grab the biggest thing that fits, right now, never look back. Works for standard coins. Can fail on other systems — always verify greedy is actually correct for the problem.

sections.title: Assign Cookies
sections.problemStatement: Each child i has a greed factor g[i] (minimum cookie size to be content). Each cookie j has a size s[j]. Assign at most one cookie per child to maximize the number of content children.
hints: Sort both g and s first — greedy needs to compare 'smallest unsatisfied need' against 'smallest available cookie' in order.
Give the LEAST greedy unsatisfied child the SMALLEST cookie that still satisfies them — using a big cookie on a low-need child wastes it on someone who could've been satisfied more cheaply.
Two pointers, one over sorted g and one over sorted s: advance the cookie pointer always; advance the child pointer (and count a match) only when the current cookie satisfies the current child.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each child (sorted by greed, least first), scan all remaining cookies for the smallest one that satisfies them.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m) for the nested scan

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort both arrays, then a single two-pointer greedy pass: the smallest available cookie either satisfies the least-greedy remaining child, or is too small for anyone and gets skipped.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n + m log m) for sorting, O(n + m) for the two-pointer pass
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space beyond the sort

sections.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of daily stock prices, find the maximum profit from buying on one day and selling on a LATER day. Return 0 if no profit is possible.
hints: Greedy choice: at every day, the best possible buy day so far is simply the lowest price seen up to that point — never reconsidered once a later, lower price appears.
Track minPriceSoFar as you scan once; at each day, the best possible profit if selling TODAY is prices[today] - minPriceSoFar.
Track a running maxProfit across all days — no need to try every buy/sell pair explicitly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every pair of buy day and later sell day, keeping the best profit found.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Single pass, greedily tracking the lowest price seen so far and the best profit possible against it.
